output:
I'd play R1f2. You were right to look for a way to activate your rooks instead of grabbing the pawn on h6, but the first rook move can be even stronger—double your rooks on the f-file and press Black’s hanging rook on d2 while keeping your own pawns defended. Black’s king is stuck in the center, so trading a pair of rooks often clarifies the pawn race and keeps your edge. You’ll likely follow up with Re3 or Re2, reinforcing control and threatening to push your kingside pawns.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s rook is loose and their king uncastled, doubling rooks on the file doubles the pressure and turns their temporary vulnerability into a lasting weakness.

I'd play Bxh7+.

You wanted to punish Black’s kingside pawn structure, but Qh5 walked into f5, which locks the bishop out and leaves your queen stranded. Bxh7+ is the thematic strike here: the bishop sacrifice opens the h-file, and after Kxh7 the queen swings in to h5 with unstoppable threats. It’s a pattern you’ve seen before—when the defender’s knight is stuck on e8, the pawn on h7 is often weak, and the queen’s entry square h5 is defended only by the king.

Takeaway: Before you plant your queen on h5, ask “is my bishop already supporting it?” If not, check whether the h7 pawn can be removed first.

I'd play Rh5.

You saw the pawn on h6 was hanging and took it, but that just opens the h-file for Black’s rooks and hands him a hook he can blast open later. Rh5 instead pins the h6-pawn down, keeps your own g5-pawn defended, and doubles the pressure on the kingside while leaving Black’s structure intact—so you stay flexible. From here the plan is simple: shuffle the other rook to the h-file, bring the knight out to g3, and only then decide whether to trade on h6 or roll the g-pawn.

Takeaway: When you spot a pawn that looks loose, ask if attacking it helps you or just helps your opponent.

I'd play Ng3. You saw a loose pawn on h6 and grabbed it right away—great instinct! But in this position, White has two rooks and a knight that are barely doing anything yet. Ng3 gets the knight out where it can breathe, defends the bishop on e2, and leaves the rooks ready to swing over to the open b-file later. The pawn on h6 isn’t going anywhere, and snatching it now just trades a side pawn while Black’s pieces are still able to coordinate.

Takeaway: When your pieces are stuck behind your pawns, look for a simple developing move before grabbing material.

I'd play Rg7. You saw the loose bishop on b3, but your rook move only attacked it and the pawn on d6—Black can just slide the bishop away and your rook has nowhere else to go. Rg7 instead attacks the g6 pawn, which can’t run, and Black’s king has to step aside next move, letting your rook swing to g5 with check. It’s a simple plan: pin a pawn to the king, then swing the rook behind it for a quick fork.

Takeaway: When you spot an undefended piece, ask first if you can attack something *both* pinned and unprotected.

I'd play Ne4. You missed a great chance to improve your worst-placed piece—your knight on a7 just sits there while your knight on c5 can leap into action. Ne4 eyes the weak pawn on c3 and suddenly White has to worry about back-rank tricks like ...h6+ followed by a rook lift. Your move f6 just blocked your own king and gave White's knight a nice square on h4 without improving your pieces.

Takeaway: Before pushing a pawn, ask: “Does this help my pieces or just shuffle air?”

I'd play Qc2. You tried to chase the knight with g4, but that just opened up your king and weakened your own position—good plans attack the opponent *without* creating holes. Qc2 hits both knights at once while keeping everything defended, and it prepares to develop the light-squared bishop next move, making your whole team work together.

Takeaway: When you see two enemy pieces on the same color, look for one move that attacks both at once—it saves time and keeps your own position solid.

I'd play Nxf7+.

You saw that the pawn on f7 is only defended by the king—this is the famous “weak f7 pawn” every beginner hears about! Instead of just nudging the bishop sideways to c3, go straight for the throat: Nxf7+ wins material right away because the rook on c7 can’t recapture without hanging the rook on f8. After Black deals with the check, you pick up the exchange or a pawn with a much better position.

Takeaway: If the enemy king still guards f7, check there first—it’s often the quickest win.

I'd play Qc2.

You went for g4, which looks aggressive but actually just weakens your kingside and leaves your queen a bit loose. Qc2 is quieter, but it hits both knights at once and prepares to castle safely—either side works now. The key idea is to keep things flexible while pressuring Black to react; after Qc2 Black often has to choose between defending the knight on e4 or the one on c6, and you’ll have time to improve your position while they scramble.

Takeaway: When both sides have active pieces, look for flexible queen moves that double duties (attack and defend) before launching pawn attacks.

I'd play Nxf7+. You missed a golden chance to open the position right where Black’s king sits. The knight check pulls the king into the open, and whichever rook Black recaptures with, you can snap it off next move—your bishop on b3 eyes the newly weak f7 square. That kind of forcing sequence is exactly what you want at this level: one sharp idea that leaves you with a clear follow-up.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s king is cornered, look for sacrifices that blast open lines for your own pieces.

I'd play bxc4. You chose b4 to kick the knight, but after ...Nb6 the pawn on c4 is still hanging and Black can grab space with ...a5-a4 next. By taking on c4 now, you force Black to recapture with the b-pawn, giving you a beautiful knight outpost on c4—just look how the knight on a3 eyes that square once the pawn is gone. The pawn on d4 is also now safely defended, so you can focus on smooth development.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended pawn, first check which recapture creates the happiest home for your pieces.

I'd play bxc4.

Your move b4 tried to kick the knight, but it just weakened your pawn chain and handed Black space. Capturing on c4 instead does two good things at once: it grabs a pawn and opens files for your knight to jump into the center next move. After bxc4 your knight can go to c4 or b5, eyeing squares Black can’t easily defend. That’s a much clearer way to fight for space than pushing a random pawn forward.

Takeaway: When you see an undefended pawn on a half-open file, ask if capturing it improves your piece activity — often it does.

I'd play Kh5.

You saw the flashy rook trade, but the real story is king activity. By stepping forward to h5 you threaten to pick up the rook on f1 with your king after Kg4, a maneuver you’ve seen in pawn-endgame studies but rarely get to use with an extra rook on the board. Your opponent can’t answer every threat: if they guard the rook, they drop a pawn; if they push a pawn, you walk in and win material or simplify to a winning king-and-pawn ending. You missed this quiet king move because the undefended rook on f1 was screaming for attention, yet ignoring it for one move is exactly what puts your opponent in zugzwang.

Takeaway: When the opponent has one overloaded piece, activating your king can be stronger than capturing what it defends.

I'd play Kh5.

Your rook on f1 is currently unprotected, so grabbing the opponent’s rook with Rxf6+ forces you to trade without solving that basic problem. Instead, simply step the king aside to h5; this keeps the rook defended, threatens to march the king across to pick up Black’s loose pawns, and gives you a clear plan—activate the king before your opponent can exploit the pin. It’s a quiet but powerful move that teaches you how to use your king as a strong piece in the endgame.

Takeaway: When your rook is loose, a quiet king move often fixes the issue while setting up your own threats.

I'd play Bb2.

Look at how crowded your queen’s path is. That pawn on c3 and the bishop on c1 are blocking her, and your knight on a3 is out of play. Moving the bishop to b2 clears the back rank, develops a piece that’s been sitting idle, and suddenly your rook on a1 is connected and ready to swing into the game. You also attack the long diagonal, which puts gentle pressure on Black’s position—no need to rush with a pawn move like b4 when simple development gives you a solid, flexible plan.

Takeaway: When your pieces feel cramped, develop the most natural one to open lines and connect your rooks first.

I'd play Rac8. You saw the knight on c5 attacking twice, but putting your rook behind it on the same file (Rac8) is cleaner than chasing it with the queen or letting White swap pieces whenever they want. This move also keeps your rook on f8 flexible to swing to the kingside if White starts pushing pawns there. You played Rfe8, which did nothing immediate—Rac8 instead adds pressure to the knight while defending your own queen.

Takeaway: When an opponent’s piece is overloaded, line up behind it before trying to chase it away.

I'd play Rac8. You spotted the knight on c5 hanging, but missed that White’s bishop is poised to jump to b5 and fork your queen and rook; Rac8 both protects the rook and keeps an eye on that bishop’s diagonal. After Rac8 White can’t safely grab on e5, so you’ll follow up with Qb6 to shore up the knight and prepare to double on the c-file. Your move was too quiet—active pieces need active plans.

Takeaway: When a piece is loose, look for the threats your opponent is hoping to set up, not just the material you can grab.

I'd play d5.

You spotted that the c3-knight is under fire, but Nb1 just tucks it away without improving anything. d5 instead strikes at the heart of Black’s camp right now: the pawn on e6 shields their bishop on b7 and keeps their d7-knight passive. Push it forward, and suddenly their whole queenside feels uncomfortable—often they’ll have to trade pawns on c3 just to untangle, which lets you recapture with your knight and keep the pressure going.

Takeaway: When a piece is attacked, look for a move that both solves the threat *and* creates a new problem for your opponent.

I'd play Re8.

You chose to grab the pawn on b2, which is natural—we all love taking material! But right now, White’s rooks are active while our own rook on e2 is just sitting there. By lifting it to e8, we ask White what they’re planning with that rook on g4 and prepare to push our king forward. The pawn on b2 isn’t going anywhere, so getting the rook into the game first is the smarter start.

Takeaway: Always glance at what your pieces are doing before grabbing pawns—active pieces beat extra material nine times out of ten.

I'd play d5.

You went for Nb1, which is a solid defensive move, but it just shuffles a piece without making progress. Pushing d5 instead takes the fight to Black’s center—it challenges the e6 pawn and opens lines for your pieces, especially the bishop on f1 and the rook on d1. Black’s pieces are a bit tangled (the knight on h8 hasn’t even moved yet), so seizing space here puts them under real pressure. You’ll often get a chance to recapture on c3 with your knight, keeping strong central control.

Takeaway: In open positions, central pawn breaks often trump passive piece moves—strike while your opponent’s pieces are still waking up.

I'd play d5. You noticed that your knight on c3 is under attack, but instead of retreating it with Nb1, you can hit back in the center right away. Pushing d5 challenges Black’s pawn on e6 and opens lines for your pieces, giving you space to develop your bishop or knight next—it’s a much more active way to deal with the threat. The idea is simple: when someone attacks a piece, look for a way to counterattack instead of just running away.

Takeaway: If your opponent attacks one of your pieces, check first if you can strike back in the center before retreating.

I'd play O-O. You tried to improve the knight, but right now the most important thing is keeping your king safe—castling does that in one move while also connecting your rooks for later teamwork. After you castle, you can still trade pawns on d4 and develop the other knight easily, but now your king is tucked away and your pieces can work together safely.

Takeaway: Castle early unless there’s a clear reason not to—it’s the quickest way to protect your king and activate your rooks.

I'd play Re8.

You saw the chance to grab the b2 pawn with your king, which is fine—it doesn’t throw away the win—but it misses a stronger way to push White’s king away. By lifting your rook to e8, you cut off the White king’s only escape square on b1 and set up a simple plan: sneak your king to b3, pick up the b2 pawn *with check*, and keep your extra rook safe. It’s a cleaner path to the same goal, and it teaches you to look for rook lifts that steal squares from the enemy king.

Takeaway: When ahead material, look for quiet rook moves that pare down the opponent’s king shelter before you cash in.

I'd play Nc3. You took on d5 right away, but that just helped Black develop their bishop next move—we want to make moves that *help us* first! Knight to c3 does three good things at once: it gets a knight out, attacks their pawn, and keeps our position safe. The real plan is simple: trade pawns *after* we’ve got more pieces ready, so we don’t hand Black a free tempo.

Takeaway: Always ask, “Does this move help me develop or defend?” before trading.

I'd play Nc3.

You took on d5 right away, but at this level the pushy queen on h4 is calling the shots. Nc3 does three useful things at once: it develops a piece, points toward the center, and threatens to snatch the d5 pawn yourself. If Black tries to defend with …Nf6 you simply recapture with the knight, forcing their knight to a worse square and kicking that queen around again. That one move puts Black on the defensive and keeps your pieces working together.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s queen is out early, prioritize moves that develop your own pieces and attack their loose pawns—this usually makes their queen regret leaving home.

I'd play Nc3.

You spotted that Black’s pawn on d5 is loose, and grabbing it is tempting. But after exd5 Black recaptures with the bishop, and suddenly the queen on h4 is staring at the white king with open lines. Nc3 attacks the same pawn, yet does something just as important: it connects the rooks and finishes development without weakening your king. If Black tries to hang on to the pawn with …Bf5 or …e6, you can simply play Nxd5 next move and still win the pawn—only now with active pieces.

Takeaway: When you see a loose pawn, ask which developing move also attacks it; often that’s the cleanest way to win material without misplacing your own pieces.

I'd play Re8. Your move Kxb2 is completely fine, but Re8 is more instructive because it shows the power of cutting off the king with a rook—you create space for your own king to advance while keeping White’s king stuck behind the a-pawn. The plan is simple: lift the rook to the 8th rank to threaten b4, then bring your king forward once White spends a move defending the b-pawn. This turnaround—using the rook actively instead of passively defending—is something advanced players should practice regularly.

Takeaway: When your king is already central, ask if your rook can create threats on an open file instead of just defending.

I'd play Rf7. You just spotted the bishop on g5 eyeing your kingside, so you tucked the king away—but rooks belong on open files, not pawns on f2! By moving the rook to f7 you immediately threaten the f2 pawn while doubling your control of the 7th rank, giving White’s queen less room to wiggle. This line also keeps your bishop on g7 defended and ready to jump into the game later.

Takeaway: When you see a piece eyeing your king, first look for active defenses that both block the threat and add your own pressure.

I'd play Rf7.

You spotted that the bishop on g7 is under attack and tried to save it by hiding the king. That’s careful, but it’s even stronger to *use* the rook that’s already on the open f-file. Moving it to f7 protects the bishop and also delivers a little checkmate-ish prod: if White ignores the rook, their pawn on f2 can fall next move, giving you more power in the center. Plus, after Rf7 you still have the option to tuck the king to h8 if needed, so you keep all your good options alive while making the board do more work for you.

Takeaway: When a piece is hanging, look for a *lift-raise* move that both saves it and adds a new threat—often you get both for the price of one.

I'd play Nbc3.

You spotted the flashy check on d6, but the real strength of White’s position is the pawn on d4asking Black to trade on d4 and open the c-file. Moving the knight to c3 keeps that tension and adds support to d4 without letting Black wriggle off the hook. It also keeps an eye on e4, so Black can’t easily challenge the center with …e5 or …d4 himself. Next move you’ll simply push e5 and your pieces will flood forward.

Takeaway: When you have a strong pawn center, reinforcing it calmly is often better than forcing immediate tricks.

I'd play Nbc3.

You saw the check on d6, which is natural—we all love checks! But here it just lets Black’s knight jump in and trade, and your own knight on b5 is stuck loose. Instead, Nbc3 plugs the knight into the game where it guards the center, protects your rook on d1, and keeps an eye on that Black pawn on d5. That’s a knight doing *two* useful jobs at once, and it sets you up to push your c-pawn next to open lines for your rooks.

Takeaway: Look for squares where a piece can defend something *and* attack something else—one move, double duty.

I'd play Rf7. You spotted the bishop hanging on g7 and moved the king to safety, which is the right instinct—keeping the king defended is crucial. But White’s queen on e6 is eyeing h6, and hiding on h8 or h7 just invites Qh3 with check, forcing you back to g8 anyway. Instead, **Rf7** steps into the action *and* builds a concrete plan: it guards the bishop on g7, protects your c7 pawn, and suddenly your queen on d4 can jump to d7 to trade queens or swing to the kingside. You're trading a passive king move for an active rook lift that immediately improves your position and prepares counterplay—exactly the kind of forcing, high-return move an advanced player should look for.

Takeaway: When your piece is attacked, look first for ways to *re-purpose* an existing piece to defend it while improving your position, rather than automatically retreating.

I'd play Rxg6+. You missed a golden opportunity to punish Black’s loose king and undefended pawns. After taking on g6 with check, your rook not only grabs another pawn on g3 but also keeps Black’s king trapped in the corner, giving you a clear plan: trade off the last pawns and win with the extra material. This is exactly the kind of forcing sequence advanced players should spot—when the opponent’s pieces are hanging, a check often reveals even bigger targets.

Takeaway: Always scan for checks when your opponent has undefended pieces near their king—it’s the fastest way to expose bigger weaknesses.

I'd play Rxg6+.

You missed a great chance to take something for free! The pawn on g6 is just hanging—it’s attacked by your rook and defended only by the Black king. When you see a pawn or piece that’s undefended or loose like this, always ask yourself: *Can I safely take it?* Here, Rxg6+ wins the pawn in one move, checks the king, and leaves you with extra material for no risk. The game gets simpler when you have more wood on the board.

Takeaway: Loose pawns and pieces are targets—before you do anything else, check whether you can take them safely.

I'd play Rxg6+.

You missed a forcing check that wins material right away. The rook lands on g6 with tempo, and after the king moves you can grab the loose pawn on g3. That’s two pawns for nothing—just look for checks when you’re up material or the opponent’s king is exposed.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s king is stuck in the open, always ask “Can I check?” first.

I'd play Bg7. You noticed White’s rook swinging on e3 could eye the pawn on f6, so you tried to block it with Nc6, but that actually lets White trade on f6 without a good recapture—your knight blocks the rook on h8. Bg7 instead solves everything in one go: it develops your bishop toward the center, gives f6 solid backup, and opens the rook on h8 so it can join the fight along the h-file. With the bishop on g7, you’re ready to castle next move and keep everything safe while connecting your rooks.

Takeaway: When you spot an undefended pawn, ask which piece can defend it *and* do one other useful job, like developing or unblocking a rook.

I'd play g4. The knight on f5 is Black’s best piece, eyeing h4 and ready to jump into e3 or d4, so kicking it away with g4 makes sense—it’s a common pawn push to challenge a strong knight. You didn’t blunder with Nd6+, but it just trades knights without really improving your position, while g4 actually fights for space and keeps Black’s pieces under pressure. The pawn on h5 can’t take en passant, so Black will have to retreat the knight or give up the h5 pawn, either way helping you seize the initiative.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a powerful knight close to your king, pushing a pawn to challenge it is often stronger than trading.

I'd play **Bg7**.

You spotted that the pawn on f6 is under fire, but moving the knight to c6 doesn’t address that—it just shuffles pieces without solving the real issue. **Bg7** develops your bishop, defends the f6 pawn *and* the rook on h8, and fits a clear plan: strengthen your kingside while keeping your structure intact. It also prepares to recapture on f6 if White trades, which keeps your position flexible and solid.

Takeaway: When a pawn is attacked, look first for ways to defend it that also improve your pieces—don’t just move the closest helper.

I'd play f5.

You spotted a knight move that looked active, but Nc6 lets White trade pawns on f6 and leave you with a backward e-pawn and a hole on e6. Pushing f5 instead keeps the pawn on f6 defended, closes the long diagonal so the bishop on d3 can’t bother you with discovered attacks, and gives your light-squared bishop the e6-square it wants. It’s a classic Advanced-French idea: fix the pawn chain toward their pawn on e5 and then aim at d4 with pieces behind it.

Takeaway: When you’re cramped, push the base of the pawn chain toward the opponent’s pawn center, not sideways; it usually relieves the pressure and sets up future breaks.

I'd play Rd6. You had a great rook on an open file, but it wasn’t doing much back on b6. Sliding it to d6 attacks Black’s pawn on a6 and also eyes the weak d5 pawn—this gives you real pressure and ties Black down. The move you played, Qf3, just adds protection to a pawn that wasn’t in any danger, while Rd6 actually creates problems for your opponent.

Takeaway: Look for open files where your rook can attack weak or undefended pawns.

I'd play **a5**.

You already understand that Black’s pawn is closer to promotion, but timing matters—pushing the pawn *now* forces White’s king to react immediately. On b3 or b4 your king stands passively, letting White’s king comb the board; a5 instead creates a direct threat and sets the tempo for the race. The idea is simple: every pawn move shrinks the number of steps White needs to reach your king, so each one should carry a concrete gain—here, cutting off the enemy king one rank earlier.

Takeaway: When races are close, push the pawn *before* tucking the king away—each advance can dictate the opponent’s rhythm.

I'd play Rd6. You just dropped your rook into the heart of Black’s position—it hits the loose a-pawn and the d5 pawn while keeping e6 defended. Instead of shuffling the queen sideways to f3, you create real tension and force Black to react: do they save the pawn on a6, defend d5, or try to kick the rook with …Ra7? Every option gives you a clear follow-up plan. This also fixes the mild mistake of Qf3, which let Black trade pawns at leisure; Rd6 keeps the initiative and turns the open b-file into a true weapon.

Takeaway: Activate your rooks along open or half-open files where they attack multiple pawns and deny the opponent easy trades.

I'd play O-O. You played Na2, which wanders off and doesn’t help your position, but castling is a simple, strong move that keeps your king safe and connects your rooks—exactly what you should be thinking about in the opening. Right now, Black’s bishops are pointing at your king, so getting it behind a wall of pawns is the smartest plan. Castling also frees your rook on h1, letting it join the game if needed.

Takeaway: Castle early unless you have a very good reason not to—it keeps your king safe and your rooks ready.

I'd play O-O.

Your Na2 blundered time trying to trade bishops on a poor square—advanced players know development beats incidental tricks. Castling now gets your king safe and your rook into the open e-file while keeping all pieces defended; the pawn storm g4 is coming next, and you’ll meet any …h6 with Na4 targeting b6. This move sets a flexible, aggressive plan without overextending a single piece.

Takeaway: At this level, prioritize king safety and piece harmony over one-move tactics when the position is still fluid.

I'd play Qf3.

Qf3 keeps an eye on d1, threatens the loose rook on a8, and eyeballs f5; if Black defends the pawn (say, …g6 or …Nf7), you can follow up with Ne4 and suddenly you’re trading pieces while dominating the dark squares. It also keeps your queen flexible enough to switch to the kingside if Black tries to counter there.

Takeaway: When the opponent’s pieces are scattered, look for queen moves that hit multiple loose targets while keeping your own team coordinated.

I'd play Qf3. You dropped the rook to d2, which just sits there, while Black’s knight on h6 is eyeing your queen—you’d have to waste a move to step away next turn. Qf3 keeps the heat on: it attacks the rook on a8, stops Black’s f-pawn from pushing, and lets you reroute the knight to e4 where it eyes f6 and d6. This move actually threatens something real, unlike Rd2 which was just marking time.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a loose piece, look for ways to add more pressure before they can regroup.

I'd play Bg5.

You played Na2, which just moves the knight to the edge without a clear follow-up. Instead, Bg5 immediately pressures Black’s knight on f6—a strong central piece. This forces Black to react (like …h6 or …d5), giving you time to improve your own position without awkward shuffling. It’s a simple, principled way to start an attack while keeping all your options open.

Takeaway: Look for ways to challenge your opponent’s most active pieces before making passive moves.

I'd play Qf3. Your Rd2 just parks the rook on a square where it doesn’t do much—it doesn’t attack anything or help defend anything. You played Ng3, which just tucks the knight away where it doesn’t help much—it doesn’t defend the loose pawn on d5, and it doesn’t challenge Black’s plans. Qd2 does two key things: it shields that pawn on d5 so Black can’t just grab it, and it also eyes the weak b4-pawn, which Black can’t easily defend. The best way to fix a loose pawn is usually to add protection while also asking your opponent what they’ll do about their own weaknesses.

Takeaway: When you spot an undefended pawn, first look for moves that defend it while also creating a second threat—often that’s your best shot at turning the tables.

I'd play Qa5.

You tried b4 to chase the knight, but that’s a bit slow—White can just move the knight away and you’ve pushed a pawn without really improving your position. Qa5 does two good things at once: it attacks the pawn on a2 (which only the queen protects) and it pins the knight on c3 to the queen, meaning the knight can’t move without hurting White’s position. If White’s queen moves, you can check on a7 next move to win the pawn back. It’s a clean way to create real pressure without making your pieces awkward.

Takeaway: Use your queen early to target loose pawns and create uncomfortable pins—your opponent often has to waste moves untangling.

I'd play b4.

You played h5, which is fine, but b4 is sharper and teaches a crucial pawn-endgame theme. The idea is to fix Black’s queenside pawns as potential weaknesses: after b4, the pawn on a5 is attacked and Black’s c5 pawn can’t safely advance. If Black takes on b4, you recapture and then push h5, gaining space on both wings—your king will always have more room to maneuver. This plan of creating targets on both sides is a bread-and-butter advanced technique in king and pawn endings.

Takeaway: In pawn endgames, create targets by fixing enemy pawns on squares where they can’t safely move.

You keep momentum and create two separate pawn races instead of one.

Takeaway: When you have a pawn majority on one side of the board, look for ways to force trades that leave your pawns free to advance.
